subject: science
prompt: Which structure controls what enters and leaves an animal cell?
explainer: The cell membrane is the gatekeeper of every cell. Plant cells have one too, with a tougher cell wall outside it.

subject: science
prompt: Which feature is in plant cells but not in animal cells?
explainer: Chloroplasts hold chlorophyll for photosynthesis. Animals don't make their own food, so animal cells don't have chloroplasts.

subject: science
prompt: Where in a cell does most respiration happen?
explainer: Mitochondria release energy from glucose. Cells that work hard, like muscle cells, have lots of mitochondria.

subject: science
prompt: What does the nucleus contain?
explainer: The nucleus stores DNA, which is the set of instructions that tells the cell how to work.

subject: science
prompt: Which microscope lens should you choose first when finding a sample?
explainer: Start at the lowest magnification to find the sample, then turn the higher lenses in once it's in view.

subject: science
prompt: A plant cell wall is made mainly of:
explainer: Cellulose makes the wall stiff so the plant can hold its shape upright.

subject: science
prompt: As a solid heats up and melts, the particles:
options: Gain energy and move further apart
explainer: Particles vibrate more, then slide past each other as a liquid. The same particles are still there — they've just got more energy.

subject: science
prompt: Which state has particles arranged in a fixed pattern?
explainer: Solids hold their shape because particles are locked in place. They vibrate but don't slide past each other.

subject: science
prompt: Why does a gas spread out to fill its container?
options: Particles move quickly in all directions
Particles are too heavy to stop
explainer: Gas particles move fast and have lots of empty space around them, so they spread until they hit the walls.

subject: science
prompt: Pure water boils at:
explainer: At normal pressure, pure water boils at 100 °C. While it's boiling, the temperature stays steady — the energy is going into changing state.

subject: science
prompt: Diffusion means particles:
options: Spread from where they're crowded to where they're not
explainer: Particles move from high concentration to low concentration. It's why a smell from the kitchen reaches the whole house.

subject: science
prompt: Which of these is a contact force?
explainer: Friction needs two surfaces touching. Gravity, magnetism and static can act without contact.

subject: science
prompt: A skydiver falls faster and faster, then reaches a steady speed. This steady speed is called:
explainer: At terminal velocity, air resistance equals weight, so the skydiver stops speeding up.

subject: science
prompt: The unit of force is the:
explainer: Forces are measured in newtons. Weight on Earth is roughly 10 N for every 1 kg of mass.

subject: science
prompt: Friction always acts:
options: In the same direction as motion
Only when something is at rest
explainer: Friction opposes motion (or attempted motion). It's why a sliding object slows down and stops.

subject: science
prompt: Energy is measured in:
explainer: All forms of energy use the same unit — the joule.

subject: science
prompt: A stretched rubber band stores energy in its:
explainer: Stretching or squashing stores elastic energy, ready to do work when released.

subject: science
prompt: When a kettle heats water, the main transfer is:
explainer: Electrical energy in the heating element becomes thermal energy in the water.

subject: science
prompt: A toy car at the top of a slope mostly stores energy in which way?
explainer: Anything raised up has gravitational potential energy. As it rolls down, this transfers to kinetic energy.

subject: science
prompt: The pitch of a sound depends on:
explainer: Higher frequency means higher pitch. Volume is set by amplitude, not pitch.

subject: science
prompt: The loudness of a sound depends on:
explainer: Bigger amplitude means louder sound. Frequency controls pitch, amplitude controls volume.

subject: science
prompt: Sound cannot travel through:
explainer: Sound needs particles to pass on the vibration. A vacuum has no particles, so no sound.

subject: science
prompt: Sound waves are:
explainer: In a sound wave, particles vibrate back and forth in the same direction the wave travels — that's longitudinal.

subject: science
prompt: An acid has a pH:
explainer: Below 7 is acidic, exactly 7 is neutral, above 7 is alkaline. The full range is usually 0 to 14.

subject: science
prompt: When an acid reacts with an alkali, the products are:
explainer: This is neutralisation. The salt's name depends on the acid — for example, hydrochloric acid makes a chloride.

subject: science
prompt: Which indicator turns red in acid and blue in alkali?
explainer: Litmus paper is the simplest acid/alkali test. Universal indicator gives a fuller colour scale for pH.

subject: science
prompt: Which household item is alkaline?
explainer: Baking-soda solution (sodium hydrogen carbonate) is mildly alkaline. The others are acidic.

subject: science
prompt: The female reproductive cell in humans is called the:
explainer: Eggs are released from an ovary. Sperm come from the testes.

subject: science
prompt: In a flower, pollen is made by the:
explainer: Anthers (on the stamens) produce pollen. The stigma is the sticky top of the female part — it catches pollen.

subject: science
prompt: After fertilisation in a flower, the ovule becomes a:
explainer: The fertilised ovule grows into a seed. The ovary around it often becomes the fruit.

subject: science
prompt: Differences between members of the same species are called:
explainer: Variation can be inherited (such as eye colour) or environmental (such as height being affected by diet).

subject: science
prompt: Which trait is mostly affected by environment, not just genes?
explainer: Diet and exercise change weight a lot. Eye colour, blood group and finger count are mainly inherited.

subject: science
prompt: On a distance-time graph, a horizontal line means the object is:
explainer: Distance isn't changing — the object is at rest. A sloped line means it's moving; a steeper slope means faster.

subject: science
prompt: On a distance-time graph, the steeper the line, the:
explainer: Steep slope = lots of distance covered in a short time = fast. Gentle slope = slow.

subject: science
prompt: Speed equals:
explainer: Speed = distance ÷ time. A car covering 60 miles in 1 hour has a speed of 60 mph.

subject: science
prompt: Which of these is a pure substance?
explainer: A pure substance has only one type of particle. Distilled water = just H₂O. The others are mixtures.

subject: science
prompt: How would you separate salt from salty water?
explainer: Heat the water until it evaporates — salt is left behind. Filtering doesn't work because salt has dissolved.

subject: science
prompt: Distillation is used to separate:
options: A pure liquid from a solution
A magnetic metal from a non-magnet
explainer: Distillation boils the liquid off, then condenses the vapour. Useful for getting pure water from salt water.

subject: science
prompt: Chromatography is mainly used to:
options: Separate different coloured dyes in an ink
explainer: Different dyes travel up the paper at different speeds, so they spread into separate spots.

subject: science
prompt: The skeleton's main jobs include:
explainer: Bones support the body's shape, protect organs (skull, ribs) and act as anchors for muscles to pull on.

subject: science
prompt: Your knee is an example of which type of joint?
explainer: Hinge joints bend one way only (like a door). The shoulder is ball-and-socket — it can rotate.

subject: science
prompt: Your biceps and triceps are an antagonistic muscle pair. This means:
options: They both work in the same direction
When one contracts, the other relaxes
explainer: Muscles can only pull, not push. So they work in pairs — one contracts to bend the arm, the other contracts to straighten it.

subject: science
prompt: The principle of conservation of energy says that energy:
options: Cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred
explainer: Energy never vanishes — it just moves between stores. A bouncing ball loses height because energy transfers to heat and sound, not because it disappears.

subject: science
prompt: Which is a renewable energy resource?
explainer: Renewables won't run out on human timescales — wind, solar, hydro, tidal, geothermal. Fossil fuels are non-renewable.

subject: science
prompt: A bouncing ball loses height each bounce because:
options: Energy transfers to heat and sound on each bounce
explainer: Total energy is the same, but some moves to thermal and sound stores at each bounce — less is left for kinetic and gravitational potential.

subject: science
prompt: When an acid reacts with a metal, the products are:
explainer: Acid + metal → salt + hydrogen. You can test for hydrogen with a lit splint — it makes a squeaky pop.

subject: science
prompt: When magnesium burns in oxygen, what is produced?
explainer: Metal + oxygen → metal oxide. The bright white flame of burning magnesium gives off a white powder of magnesium oxide.

subject: science
prompt: In a displacement reaction, a more reactive metal:
options: Joins onto a less reactive metal
Pushes a less reactive metal out of its compound
explainer: More reactive metals "push out" less reactive ones. Iron + copper sulfate → iron sulfate + copper.

subject: science
prompt: An echo is caused by:
options: Sound bouncing off a hard surface
explainer: When sound hits a hard surface, it reflects. We hear the reflection a moment later as an echo.

subject: science
prompt: Which part of the ear vibrates first when sound enters?
explainer: Sound waves travel down the ear canal and make the eardrum vibrate. Tiny bones then carry the vibration to the cochlea.

subject: science
prompt: Continuous variation means:
options: There are only two possible options
Values fall along a smooth scale (like height)
explainer: Continuous: any value in a range (height, weight). Discontinuous: only specific options (blood group, eye colour).

subject: science
prompt: How many planets orbit our Sun?
explainer: Eight: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune. Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.

subject: science
prompt: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
explainer: Mercury — about 58 million km from the Sun. So close that surface temperatures swing wildly between day and night.

subject: science
prompt: Day and night happen because:
options: The Sun moves around the Earth
The Earth spins on its axis
explainer: Earth makes one full spin every 24 hours. The side facing the Sun has day; the side facing away has night.

subject: science
prompt: What causes the seasons on Earth?
options: The Earth's distance from the Sun changing
The Earth's axis being tilted as it orbits the Sun
explainer: Earth's axis is tilted ~23.5°. As we orbit the Sun, different hemispheres point towards it — giving longer, warmer days in summer.

subject: science
prompt: Roughly how long does it take Earth to orbit the Sun once?
explainer: 365.25 days — about a year. The extra 0.25 is why we add a leap day every 4 years.

subject: science
prompt: Phases of the Moon (new, crescent, full…) happen because:
options: We see different amounts of the Moon's lit side as it orbits Earth
Earth's shadow falls on the Moon every night
Clouds cover parts of the Moon
explainer: The Sun always lights half the Moon. As the Moon orbits Earth, we see different proportions of that lit half — full, half, crescent, new.
